title: 20 Tahsas — The First Entry into Egypt & The Assembly
narrative:
The twentieth day of Tahsas commemorates the First Entry into Egypt, that mysterious episode recorded in the apocryphal Gospel of the Infancy and treasured especially in the Coptic and Ethiopian traditions, wherein the Holy Family — Joseph, Mary, and the Christ Child — fled the fury of Herod and entered the land of Egypt, fulfilling the word of the Prophet Hosea: 'Out of Egypt have I called my Son.' The Egyptian Church received this mystery with particular tenderness, for it meant that the land which had once enslaved the children of Israel had itself become the refuge of the Lord of Israel; and every Coptic church that stands over a site where the Holy Family rested bears witness to the paradox that the exile of the Messiah became the consecration of the land of His exile. The feast commemorates not merely a flight but a sanctification — the holy feet of the Child Who Is God walking upon the dust of Egypt and making it holy ground.

Saint Ignatius of Antioch, the great second-century bishop and martyr whose name is believed by some scholars to derive from the Latin igneus, meaning 'fiery,' was himself a man of burning devotion and apostolic fire. Ignatius was condemned to be thrown to the beasts in the arena at Rome under the Emperor Trajan, and the seven letters he wrote during his journey to martyrdom under guard constitute the most passionate and theologically fertile epistolary witness in the sub-apostolic era. Ignatius coined the term 'catholica' for the universal Church, insisted with a vehemence born of mystical intuition on the full humanity and full divinity of Christ, and longed for his death with a fervor that later centuries would sometimes misread as morbid but that was in fact the overflow of a love so great that the earthly life had become too small to contain it.

Saint John of Kronstadt, the beloved Russian priest and wonder-worker of the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries whose feast the Ethiopian Synaxarium includes in its expanded calendar, was a man whose extraordinary life of liturgical prayer and pastoral charity made him one of the most recognized spiritual figures of his era. His name John — 'the Lord is gracious' — he bore worthily, for the divine grace flowed through him in a constant stream of healing miracles, pastoral counsel, and charitable works that reached across the vastness of the Russian empire. His diary, published as My Life in Christ, stands as one of the great documents of eucharistic theology and daily spiritual combat.

The theological seal of this twentieth day of Tahsas is the mystery of the sanctification of exile. The Holy Family does not flee Egypt as an emergency but sanctifies Egypt as a mission; Ignatius does not travel to Rome as a prisoner but journeys there as a witness; John of Kronstadt does not merely serve his parish but transforms the whole of his society by the radiation of the divine love he carries within him. In each case, what appears to be a limitation — flight, imprisonment, a remote parish — becomes the very site of the greatest fruitfulness. The God who sanctified Egypt by His exile will sanctify every exile and every wilderness with the warmth of His presence.
